reason: The text describes an ongoing/present disaster (a great famine in Samaria due to the Aramean siege) that affects the general public, but this destruction is portrayed as the current situation ('And there was a great famine in Samaria'), not as an upcoming event. The explicitly 'upcoming' event mentioned in the passage is Elisha's prophecy ('at this time tomorrow'), which announces relief from the famine - an abundance of grain - not a destruction. While the siege and famine can be attributed to divine judgment, they are presented as the present crisis, not a future one. The passage's narrative movement is from crisis (present famine) to resolution (upcoming abundance).
